front_text: Preeclampsia is a pregnancy-specific hypertensive disorder that develops after 20 weeks of gestation and can occur up to 6 weeks postpartum.
front_html: Preeclampsia is a pregnancy-specific hypertensive disorder that develops after {{c1::20}} weeks of gestation and can occur up to {{c1::6}} weeks postpartum.
tags: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health,Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20%,Eclampsia/HELLP
curriculum_topic_path: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health > Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20% > Eclampsia/HELLP

front_text: Preeclampsia is defined by new-onset hypertension (blood pressure ≥140/90 mmHg) and proteinuria (≥300 mg in 24 hours or ≥+1 on dipstick)
front_html: Preeclampsia is defined by new-onset {{c1::hypertension}} (blood pressure ≥{{c1::140/90}} mmHg) and {{c1::proteinuria}} (≥{{c1::300 mg}} in 24 hours or ≥{{c1::+1}} on dipstick)
tags: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health,Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20%,Eclampsia/HELLP
curriculum_topic_path: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health > Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20% > Eclampsia/HELLP

front_text: Mild preeclampsia: blood pressure ranges between 140/90 mmHg and 160/110 mmHg, with proteinuria greater than 300 mg in 24 hours or a dipstick reading of +1.
front_html: Mild preeclampsia: blood pressure ranges between {{c1::140/90}} mmHg and {{c1::160/110}} mmHg, with proteinuria greater than {{c1::300}} mg in 24 hours or a dipstick reading of {{c1::+1}}.
tags: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health,Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20%,Eclampsia/HELLP
extra:
Other mild preeclampsia features:
• edema (hands, face, lower extremities)
• most patients asymptomatic
• some report mild headaches or visual disturbances.
curriculum_topic_path: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health > Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20% > Eclampsia/HELLP

front_text: Mild preeclampsia: Experience edema, particularly in the hands, feet, and lower extremities.  Most patients are asymptomatic, though some may report mild headaches or visual disturbances.
front_html: Mild preeclampsia: Experience edema, particularly in the {{c1::hands}}, {{c1::feet}}, and {{c1::lower extremities}}. Most patients are {{c1::asymptomatic}}, though some may report mild headaches or visual disturbances.
tags: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health,Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20%,Eclampsia/HELLP
extra:
Other mild preeclampsia features:
• BP 140/90–160/110 mmHg
• proteinuria >300 mg/24h or +1 dipstick.
curriculum_topic_path: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health > Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20% > Eclampsia/HELLP

front_text: The only definitive cure for preeclampsia is delivery of the fetus and placenta, because the condition originates from placental dysfunction.
front_html: The only definitive cure for preeclampsia is {{c1::delivery}} of the fetus and placenta, because the condition originates from {{c1::placental dysfunction}}.
tags: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health,Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20%,Eclampsia/HELLP
curriculum_topic_path: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health > Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20% > Eclampsia/HELLP

front_text: Mild preeclampsia management: for patients at ≥37 weeks gestation, induction of labor is recommended to prevent progression.
front_html: Mild preeclampsia management: for patients at ≥{{c1::37}} weeks gestation, {{c1::induction of labor}} is recommended to prevent progression.
tags: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health,Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20%,Eclampsia/HELLP
extra: Other mild preeclampsia management: if diagnosed around 26–30 weeks, administer antenatal corticosteroids (betamethasone) to accelerate fetal lung maturity before delivery.
curriculum_topic_path: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health > Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20% > Eclampsia/HELLP

front_text: Mild preeclampsia management: if diagnosed around 26–30 weeks gestation, antenatal corticosteroids such as betamethasone are administered to accelerate fetal lung maturity before delivery.
front_html: Mild preeclampsia management: if diagnosed around {{c1::26–30}} weeks gestation, {{c1::antenatal corticosteroids}} such as {{c1::betamethasone}} are administered to accelerate fetal lung maturity before delivery.
tags: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health,Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20%,Eclampsia/HELLP
extra: Other mild preeclampsia management: at ≥37 weeks, induction of labor is recommended.
curriculum_topic_path: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health > Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20% > Eclampsia/HELLP

front_text: Severe preeclampsia is characterized by blood pressure greater than 160/110 mmHg and proteinuria exceeding 5 g in 24 hours or ≥+3 on dipstick.
front_html: Severe preeclampsia is characterized by blood pressure greater than {{c1::160/110}} mmHg and proteinuria exceeding {{c1::5}} g in 24 hours or ≥{{c1::+3}} on dipstick.
tags: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health,Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20%,Eclampsia/HELLP
extra: Other severe preeclampsia features: severe headaches, visual disturbances, right upper quadrant or epigastric pain (liver involvement), pulmonary edema, decreased urine output.
curriculum_topic_path: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health > Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20% > Eclampsia/HELLP

front_text: Severe preeclampsia systemic symptoms include severe headaches, visual disturbances, right upper quadrant or epigastric pain (reflecting liver involvement), pulmonary edema, and decreased urine output.
front_html: Severe preeclampsia systemic symptoms include severe headaches, {{c1::visual disturbances}}, {{c1::right upper quadrant}} or {{c1::epigastric pain}} (reflecting liver involvement), {{c1::pulmonary edema}}, and {{c1::decreased urine output}}.
tags: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health,Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20%,Eclampsia/HELLP
extra:
Other severe preeclampsia features:
• BP >160/110 mmHg
• proteinuria >5 g/24h or ≥+3 dipstick.
curriculum_topic_path: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health > Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20% > Eclampsia/HELLP

front_text: Severe preeclampsia management: the cornerstone of treatment is intravenous magnesium sulfate, which prevents the onset of seizures.
front_html: Severe preeclampsia management: the cornerstone of treatment is intravenous {{c1::magnesium sulfate}}, which prevents the onset of {{c1::seizures}}.
tags: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health,Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20%,Eclampsia/HELLP
extra:
Other severe preeclampsia management: • BP control with hydralazine or labetalol if >180/110 mmHg
• delivery at 34–36 weeks
• corticosteroids if <34 weeks.
curriculum_topic_path: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health > Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20% > Eclampsia/HELLP

front_text: Severe preeclampsia management: blood pressure should be controlled with hydralazine or labetalol, especially if it exceeds 180/110 mmHg.
front_html: Severe preeclampsia management: blood pressure should be controlled with {{c1::hydralazine}} or {{c1::labetalol}}, especially if it exceeds {{c1::180/110}} mmHg.
tags: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health,Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20%,Eclampsia/HELLP
extra:
Other severe preeclampsia management:
• IV magnesium sulfate for seizure prevention
• delivery at 34–36 weeks
• corticosteroids if <34 weeks.
curriculum_topic_path: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health > Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20% > Eclampsia/HELLP

front_text: Severe preeclampsia management: delivery is typically performed between 34 and 36 weeks gestation, depending on maternal and fetal stability.
front_html: Severe preeclampsia management: delivery is typically performed between {{c1::34}} and {{c1::36}} weeks gestation, depending on maternal and fetal stability.
tags: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health,Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20%,Eclampsia/HELLP
extra:
Other severe preeclampsia management:
• IV magnesium sulfate
• BP control with hydralazine or labetalol
• corticosteroids if <34 weeks.
curriculum_topic_path: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health > Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20% > Eclampsia/HELLP

front_text: Severe preeclampsia management: if the pregnancy is less than 34 weeks, corticosteroids are administered to promote fetal lung maturity before delivery.
front_html: Severe preeclampsia management: if the pregnancy is less than {{c1::34}} weeks, {{c1::corticosteroids}} are administered to promote fetal lung maturity before delivery.
tags: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health,Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20%,Eclampsia/HELLP
extra:
Other severe preeclampsia management:
• IV magnesium sulfate
• BP control with hydralazine or labetalol
• delivery at 34–36 weeks.
curriculum_topic_path: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health > Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20% > Eclampsia/HELLP

front_text: Eclampsia occurs when a patient with preeclampsia develops seizures or coma not attributable to another cause, making seizures the key distinguishing feature between eclampsia and preeclampsia.
front_html: Eclampsia occurs when a patient with preeclampsia develops {{c1::seizures}} or {{c1::coma}} not attributable to another cause, making {{c1::seizures}} the key distinguishing feature between eclampsia and preeclampsia.
tags: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health,Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20%,Eclampsia/HELLP
curriculum_topic_path: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health > Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20% > Eclampsia/HELLP

front_text: Eclampsia is believed to result from cerebral vasospasm, ischemia, and edema, which lower the seizure threshold.
front_html: Eclampsia is believed to result from cerebral {{c1::vasospasm}}, {{c1::ischemia}}, and {{c1::edema}}, which lower the {{c1::seizure threshold}}.
tags: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health,Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20%,Eclampsia/HELLP
curriculum_topic_path: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health > Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20% > Eclampsia/HELLP

front_text: Eclampsia management: the first priority is maternal stabilization, securing the airway, breathing, and circulation.
front_html: Eclampsia management: the first priority is maternal stabilization, securing the {{c1::airway}}, {{c1::breathing}}, and {{c1::circulation}}.
tags: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health,Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20%,Eclampsia/HELLP
extra:
Other eclampsia management:
• IV magnesium sulfate (first-line to terminate seizures and prevent recurrence)
• BP control with hydralazine or labetalol
• prompt delivery once stabilized.
curriculum_topic_path: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health > Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20% > Eclampsia/HELLP

front_text: Eclampsia management: first-line treatment is intravenous magnesium sulfate, used to both terminate ongoing seizures and prevent recurrence.
front_html: Eclampsia management: first-line treatment is intravenous {{c1::magnesium sulfate}}, used to both terminate ongoing seizures and prevent {{c1::recurrence}}.
tags: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health,Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20%,Eclampsia/HELLP
extra:
Other eclampsia management:
• secure airway/breathing/circulation
• BP control with hydralazine or labetalol
• prompt delivery once stabilized.
curriculum_topic_path: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health > Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20% > Eclampsia/HELLP

front_text: Eclampsia management: blood pressure is managed with hydralazine or labetalol, and once the patient is stabilized, prompt delivery of the fetus is indicated as the only curative treatment.
front_html: Eclampsia management: blood pressure is managed with {{c1::hydralazine}} or {{c1::labetalol}}, and once the patient is stabilized, prompt {{c1::delivery}} of the fetus is indicated as the only curative treatment.
tags: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health,Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20%,Eclampsia/HELLP
extra:
Other eclampsia management:
• secure airway/breathing/circulation
• IV magnesium sulfate first-line.
curriculum_topic_path: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health > Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20% > Eclampsia/HELLP
